title: Clarifying Your Ideal Client
summary: Erin Stetzer led a session on clarifying ideal clients across different stages of business, framing clients broadly to include architects, designers, and consultants as well as direct clients. The session covered how to evaluate fit through the lens of people, time, and money, and opened into a rich Q&A where participants worked through real challenges including budget misalignment, pre-construction agreements, and transitioning to higher-end markets.

teachings.explanation: Clients are not just the people hiring you — they include architects, designers, and consultants on the project. Treating all project professionals as clients means finding ways to support their needs and make their jobs easier, which moves the project forward and builds lasting professional relationships.
teachings.quote: Clients to me are architects, are designers, consultants, and clients. These are all our clients.

teachings.explanation: Every project and client relationship should be evaluated through three lenses: the people you want to work with, the time horizon and pace that suits your personality and business model, and the money equation including cashflow, fees, and project duration. Clarity on all three is required to identify your true ideal client.
teachings.quote: When it comes to clarifying our ideal client, it's so much more than just the person hiring you. It's the time, it's the people, it's the money.

teachings.explanation: After 5–20 projects, builders can identify recurring patterns in client behavior, engagement levels, and project dynamics. Recognizing these patterns — such as when certain clients disengage during drywall phase — allows builders to proactively manage relationships and refine who they serve best.
teachings.quote: I think it's a really good idea to also recognize the patterns of people during projects.

teachings.explanation: The ideal client strategy changes with business maturity. In years 0–5, the priority is to take every project and learn. In years 5–10, patterns emerge that allow filtering. By 10–20 years, builders can be highly selective and build deep relationships with a small number of design partners to control project quality.
teachings.quote: Zero to five years into this business, it is take every project, learn, learn, learn, make it happen. Because when you have a chance to analyze five to 10 projects, you become much clearer on the clients you will serve.

teachings.explanation: Using scorecards for every project manager, architect, designer, and client creates a structured feedback loop that accelerates business clarity. Consistently scoring all project participants reveals who you work well with and compresses years of learning into actionable data.
teachings.quote: I score them, every designer we work with, every architect we work with, every client that we work with over and over and over again, because that's going to give you feedback. I promise you, if you do this, you speed yourself up five years.

teachings.concept: Price Certainty as a Process, Not a One-Time Presentation
teachings.explanation: The initial pricing conversation is the beginning of a multi-step process, not a final pitch. Framing it this way removes desperation, builds trust, and allows both parties to discover alignment or misalignment early. The goal is to reach 85–90% budget certainty before proceeding.
teachings.quote: I always thought like this is my last meeting, this is my last shot. And people feel that. But if you can also share like, this is exercise one, and this is how it looks on exercise two if we want to continue having a conversation.

teachings.explanation: Builders can take control of their client pipeline by deliberately selecting and nurturing relationships with a small number of top architects and designers. Working your way into their trusted builder circle — even through difficult projects — creates a reliable source of pre-qualified, well-matched clients.
teachings.quote: I identified, I literally picked five architects and made sure I worked with them, and five designers. Because I didn't want to deal with all the other stuff.

teachings.concept: Walking Clients Through Completed Homes to Justify Value
teachings.explanation: Taking prospective clients on a tour of a completed project and highlighting specific craft decisions — like kerf door jambs or custom floor patterns — shifts the conversation from abstract budget numbers to tangible value. This experiential approach helps clients understand why costs are what they are and builds emotional buy-in.
teachings.quote: If you're at home and you say, hey, look at this wood floor pattern, we made sure when we did this pattern we had sample sizes — and now they're like, oh, I would pay for that. Like I got it now. There's a reason behind this number.

actionable_advice: Use scorecards for every project manager, architect, designer, and client on every project to build a feedback system that clarifies your ideal client profile over time.
Evaluate every project through the people, time, and money framework before committing — not just the personality of the client.
Run your own internal cost estimate exercise before any client meeting, even if you don't share the number, so you go in informed and can make a grounded decision about whether to pursue the project.
When a project comes in over budget, present a list of 10 scope items that can be refined or removed, and walk the client through each one to tick the certainty percentage up from 75% toward 85–90%.
Before entering formal pre-construction, have a direct budget conversation upfront and do not proceed if the gap between the client's budget and your estimate exceeds roughly 25%.

To break into a new architect's or designer's stable, take their difficult or less desirable projects first, and make sure the firm's principal sees your work on site directly — not just through a project manager.
When transitioning to a higher-end market, audit your website, testimonials, past client references, and marketing to ensure they reflect where you are going, not where you came from.

Charge a meaningful, robust pre-construction fee — ideally structured with hourly rates and a significant upfront retainer — to signal seriousness and filter out clients who are shopping rather than committed.

Run pre-construction as its own project in your project management system with a Gantt chart, bi-weekly meetings, and active coordination of all team members to demonstrate value and discourage clients from taking your work to another builder.
Consider requiring a non-refundable schedule hold deposit at the point a project goes to permit, to protect your capacity and formalize commitment before breaking ground.
client_challenges: A participant building at the top end of their market struggles to guide clients through the 'over budget' phase of new builds — where plans come back significantly over what clients hoped to spend.

Denise received a referral for a complex historic district project involving two estate homes, litigation history, gut renovations, additions, and a pool, and is unsure how much information to share upfront without giving away work for free or losing the project to price shoppers.

David in Boulder is experiencing a consistent pattern where prospective clients believe a project will cost half of what it actually will, due to an influx of inexperienced architects post-Marshall fire and post-COVID who are designing homes without understanding current build costs.
David is uncertain whether to qualify budget alignment aggressively in the first meeting or allow the relationship to develop before surfacing cost reality.
Brett's firm is having pre-construction agreements signed but is still experiencing shopping behavior — clients using the pre-construction work to solicit bids from other builders at a lower price.

Tiffany asked whether participants are charging for pricing exercises and pre-construction work, and shared her own experience of nervously but successfully setting firm financial and value boundaries with a client.

A participant is unclear on the right deliverables and structure for a pre-construction agreement, particularly the timing of design selections and when it is appropriate for clients to speak to other builders.
notable_quotes: On knowing yourself to serve clients well: 'You can only serve people well when you know who you are.'
On emotional attachment to projects: 'Don't get emotionally attached to projects. You just can't.'

On transitioning to higher-end work: 'Those are the people I want to attract, not the friend of the person I built with 10 years ago because they loved me when I was building at $300 a square foot. I'm not that person anymore.'
On the danger of competing on price: 'I tried to build to that cost and I'd have crummy trades and it was miserable. And then I said, I'm done with that. I get to pick. Period.'

On pre-construction commitment: 'I want you to be serious. I'm not looking to get $500 so I can go price a set of schematic drawings. I want you to really go talk to three other builders — and if we're going to go into pre-construction, it's because you want to work with me.'

On transparency in pricing: 'I'm very clear on their direction. They're very clear on my direction. Yes, we're higher than their goals — but you better believe I have a list of 10 things I can walk through with them.'
